extra.reasoning:
The monotonicity property is violated. Conflict resolution uses per-output priority where the highest-priority matching rule wins. Rule r3 (IF severe_headache THEN acuity := self_care) has priority 100, which dominates r1 (IF focal_weakness THEN acuity := emergent, priority 3) and r2 (priority 3).

Take the baseline input severe_headache=false, focal_weakness=true, leg_swelling=false: only r1 matches, setting acuity := emergent (highest acuity).

Now set severe_headache=true with everything else unchanged (severe_headache=true, focal_weakness=true, leg_swelling=false): r1 matches (emergent, priority 3) and r3 matches (self_care, priority 100). Since 100 > 3, r3 wins and acuity := self_care (lowest acuity).

Going from severe_headache=false to true lowered acuity from emergent to self_care, directly violating the requirement that setting severe_headache true never lowers acuity. The high priority assigned to r3 inverts the intended safety direction (clinically: a severe headache with focal weakness, a stroke red flag, gets downgraded to self_care). The witness assignment is the post-flip state that exhibits the violation; the comparison baseline is the identical input with severe_headache=false.
